Final Project¶

Heart disease prediction from clinical features¶

Course: Machine Learning and Deep Learning for Healthcare | M-D6410YE
Students: Alessandro Carnio, Federico Panico

Imports and paths¶

In [2]:
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from pathlib import Path

from sklearn.model_selection import (
    train_test_split,
    GroupKFold,
    cross_validate,
    cross_val_predict,
)
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, OneHotEncoder
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer

from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.naive_bayes import GaussianNB
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier, GradientBoostingClassifier, StackingClassifier
from sklearn.svm import SVC
from sklearn.neural_network import MLPClassifier

from sklearn.metrics import (
    accuracy_score,
    f1_score,
    roc_auc_score,
    classification_report,
    brier_score_loss,
)
from sklearn.calibration import calibration_curve

sns.set_theme(style='whitegrid')
DATA_DIR = Path('data')
RANDOM_STATE = 42

1. Exploratory Data Analysis¶

We list files under data/ and read the UCI notes so we know which processed file is recommended and what each column means.

In [3]:
all_files = sorted([p for p in DATA_DIR.rglob('*') if p.is_file()])
file_table = pd.DataFrame({
    'file': [str(p.relative_to(DATA_DIR)) for p in all_files],
    'size_kb': [round(p.stat().st_size / 1024, 2) for p in all_files]
}).sort_values('size_kb', ascending=False)

file_table

file size_kb

Processed .data files are small and ready to load; raw .data dumps are larger. For modelling we rely on the processed files, as the accompanying notes suggest.

In [4]:
names_text = (DATA_DIR / 'heart-disease.names').read_text(encoding='utf-8', errors='ignore')
warning_text = (DATA_DIR / 'WARNING').read_text(encoding='utf-8', errors='ignore')

print('Excerpt from heart-disease.names (first lines):')
print('\n'.join(names_text.splitlines()[:22]))
print('\n' + '=' * 72 + '\n')
print('WARNING file:')
print(warning_text)

Excerpt from heart-disease.names (first lines):
Publication Request: 
   >>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>>
   This file describes the contents of the heart-disease directory.

   This directory contains 4 databases concerning heart disease diagnosis.
   All attributes are numeric-valued.  The data was collected from the
   four following locations:

     1. Cleveland Clinic Foundation (cleveland.data)
     2. Hungarian Institute of Cardiology, Budapest (hungarian.data)
     3. V.A. Medical Center, Long Beach, CA (long-beach-va.data)
     4. University Hospital, Zurich, Switzerland (switzerland.data)

   Each database has the same instance format.  While the databases have 76
   raw attributes, only 14 of them are actually used.  Thus I've taken the
   liberty of making 2 copies of each database: one with all the attributes
   and 1 with the 14 attributes actually used in past experiments.

   The authors of the databases have requested:

      ...that any publications resulting from the use of the data include the 
      names of the principal investigator responsible for the data collection

========================================================================

WARNING file:
The file cleveland.data has been unfortunately messed up when we lost
node cip2 and loaded the file on node ics.  The file processed.cleveland.data
seems to be in good shape and is useable (for the 14 attributes situation).
I'll clean up cleveland.data as soon as possible.

Bad news: my original copy of the database appears to be corrupted.
I'll have to go back to the donor to get a new copy.

David Aha

The warning file tells us to avoid corrupted raw extracts and to prefer the processed Cleveland file for standard analyses. We keep that in mind when choosing Cleveland as the main single-centre benchmark.

The UCI heart disease studies use the same 14 input attributes plus the diagnosis label num.

In [5]:
columns = [
    'age', 'sex', 'cp', 'trestbps', 'chol', 'fbs', 'restecg',
    'thalach', 'exang', 'oldpeak', 'slope', 'ca', 'thal', 'num'
]

numeric_cols = ['age', 'trestbps', 'chol', 'thalach', 'oldpeak']
categorical_cols = ['sex', 'cp', 'fbs', 'restecg', 'exang', 'slope', 'ca', 'thal']
pd.DataFrame({
    'name': columns,
    'type': ['numeric' if c in numeric_cols else ('label' if c == 'num' else 'categorical') for c in columns]
})
Out[5]:
name type
0 age numeric
1 sex categorical
2 cp categorical
3 trestbps numeric
4 chol numeric
5 fbs categorical
6 restecg categorical
7 thalach numeric
8 exang categorical
9 oldpeak numeric
10 slope categorical
11 ca categorical
12 thal categorical
13 num label

We will impute and scale numerics, and impute plus one-hot encode categoricals. That matches the mixed data types and avoids treating coded categories as ordered numbers.

1.1 Loading all four processed sites¶

Each row is one patient; num is the severity label (0 = no disease, 1-4 = disease present with increasing severity). For binary classification we use presence vs absence: disease if num > 0.

In [6]:
site_files = {
    'cleveland': 'processed.cleveland.data',
    'hungarian': 'processed.hungarian.data',
    'switzerland': 'processed.switzerland.data',
    'va': 'processed.va.data',
}

site_data = {}
for site, file_name in site_files.items():
    d = pd.read_csv(DATA_DIR / file_name, names=columns, header=None, na_values='?')
    d['site'] = site
    d['target_binary'] = (d['num'] > 0).astype(int)
    site_data[site] = d

full_processed = pd.concat(site_data.values(), ignore_index=True)
full_processed.shape
Out[6]:
(920, 16)
In [ ]:
summary_rows = []
for site, d in site_data.items():
    summary_rows.append({
        'site': site,
        'rows': len(d),
        'missing_total': int(d[columns].isna().sum().sum()),
        'binary_positive_rate': round(d['target_binary'].mean(), 3),
        'num_mean': round(d['num'].mean(), 3),
    })

eda_site_summary = pd.DataFrame(summary_rows).sort_values('rows', ascending=False)
eda_site_summary
Out[ ]:
site rows missing_total binary_positive_rate num_mean
0 cleveland 303 6 0.459 0.937
1 hungarian 294 782 0.361 0.361
3 va 200 698 0.745 1.520
2 switzerland 123 273 0.935 1.805

Cleveland stands out with almost no missing fields; Hungarian, VA, and Switzerland have far more gaps. Positive rate also differs a lot by site, so pooling patients blindly mixes different populations and lab situations.

In [ ]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 4))

sns.barplot(data=eda_site_summary, x='site', y='rows', ax=axes[0], color='steelblue')
axes[0].set_title('Samples per site')

sns.barplot(data=eda_site_summary, x='site', y='missing_total', ax=axes[1], color='salmon')
axes[1].set_title('Missing values per site')

sns.barplot(data=eda_site_summary, x='site', y='binary_positive_rate', ax=axes[2], color='seagreen')
axes[2].set_title('Positive rate (binary) per site')
axes[2].set_ylim(0, 1)

for ax in axes:
    ax.tick_params(axis='x', rotation=20)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The bar plots shows this imbalance: Switzerland has very few rows but a very high positive rate compared with Hungarian data, for example. That is exactly why later we separate centres when we measure how well a model travels.

In [ ]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
sns.countplot(data=full_processed, x='num', hue='site')
plt.title('Severity class (num) by site')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Severity classes are uneven and not equally represented across sites. Binary aggregation (num > 0) is what we use for the main classification task; multiclass severity would need even more care with class imbalance.

1.2 Cleeveland Site Analysis¶

We go deeper on Cleveland because it is the recommended processed subset, has minimal missingness, and matches what most papers report. The plots below are only for learning the data shape before fitting models.

In [ ]:
df = site_data['cleveland'].copy()

print('Shape:', df.shape)
print('\nMissing by column:')
print(df[columns].isna().sum().sort_values(ascending=False))
print('\nBinary target counts:')
print(df['target_binary'].value_counts())
print('\nSeverity (num) counts:')
print(df['num'].value_counts().sort_index())

Shape: (303, 16)

Only a handful of cells are missing overall; most problems concentrate in ca and thal. The binary outcome is slightly tilted but still workable without aggressive resampling for a classroom baseline.

In [ ]:
df[numeric_cols].describe().T
Out[ ]:
count mean std min 25% 50% 75% max
age 303.0 54.438944 9.038662 29.0 48.0 56.0 61.0 77.0
trestbps 303.0 131.689769 17.599748 94.0 120.0 130.0 140.0 200.0
chol 303.0 246.693069 51.776918 126.0 211.0 241.0 275.0 564.0

Ranges look clinically plausible for this dataset; nothing stands out as an obvious data entry bug at this scale.

In [ ]:
missing_rate = (df[columns].isna().mean() * 100).sort_values(ascending=False)
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 4))
sns.barplot(x=missing_rate.index, y=missing_rate.values, color='firebrick')
plt.title('Missing rate (%) per feature - Cleveland')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.ylabel('Missing %')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Almost all gaps sit in two variables, which tells us where imputation matters most for Cleveland.

In [ ]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(14, 8))
axes = axes.ravel()

for i, col in enumerate(numeric_cols):
    sns.histplot(data=df, x=col, hue='target_binary', kde=True, bins=18, ax=axes[i])
    axes[i].set_title(col)

axes[-1].axis('off')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Distributions overlap between classes, but some variables shift between healthy and disease; especially exercise-related signals, so there is signal for a learner.

In [14]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(14, 8))
axes = axes.ravel()

for i, col in enumerate(numeric_cols):
    sns.boxplot(data=df, x='target_binary', y=col, ax=axes[i])
    axes[i].set_title(col)

axes[-1].axis('off')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Boxplots highlight separation and overlap at the same time; no single measurement separates the groups alone.

In [15]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 4, figsize=(16, 8))
axes = axes.ravel()

for i, col in enumerate(categorical_cols):
    ctab = pd.crosstab(df[col], df['target_binary'], normalize='index').fillna(0)
    ctab.columns = ['rate_0', 'rate_1']
    ctab = ctab.reset_index().melt(id_vars=col, var_name='target_side', value_name='rate')
    sns.barplot(data=ctab, x=col, y='rate', hue='target_side', ax=axes[i])
    axes[i].set_title(col)
    axes[i].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=45)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Several categorical levels line up with higher disease rate, which supports encoding them properly instead of dropping them.

In [ ]:
corr = df[numeric_cols + ['target_binary']].corr(numeric_only=True)
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 6))
sns.heatmap(corr, annot=True, fmt='.2f', cmap='coolwarm', center=0)
plt.title('Correlation heatmap (numeric features and binary target)')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Correlations are moderate. We still expect gains from combining features in a model rather than using one variable in isolation.

In [17]:
pair_df = df[numeric_cols + ['target_binary']].dropna().copy()
sns.pairplot(
    pair_df,
    vars=['age', 'thalach', 'oldpeak', 'chol'],
    hue='target_binary',
    corner=True,
    plot_kws={'alpha': 0.6, 's': 18},
)
plt.show()

Pairwise scatter plots show partial separation with overlap, which matches the choice of both linear and flexible classifiers later on.

2. Models (Cleveland)¶

We fit models on Cleveland alone for a clear benchmark: same preprocessing as in standard homework setups, stratified train-test split, and several algorithms side by side including a stacking ensemble that combines logistic regression, random forest, and SVM. Cleveland is the natural choice here because it has the fewest missing values and matches the documentation.

The following steps drop the site column (every row is Cleveland) and build dense matrices after preprocessing for all sklearn estimators that expect numeric arrays.

In [18]:
df_c = site_data['cleveland'].copy()
df_c['target_multiclass'] = df_c['num'].astype(int)

X_c = df_c.drop(columns=['num', 'target_binary', 'target_multiclass', 'site'])
y_c = df_c['target_binary']

num_pipe_c = Pipeline([
    ('imputer', SimpleImputer(strategy='median')),
    ('scaler', StandardScaler()),
])
cat_pipe_c = Pipeline([
    ('imputer', SimpleImputer(strategy='most_frequent')),
    ('onehot', OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown='ignore')),
])

preprocessor_c = ColumnTransformer([
    ('num', num_pipe_c, numeric_cols),
    ('cat', cat_pipe_c, categorical_cols),
])

Xc_train, Xc_test, yc_train, yc_test = train_test_split(
    X_c, y_c, test_size=0.2, stratify=y_c, random_state=RANDOM_STATE
)

Xc_train_pre = preprocessor_c.fit_transform(Xc_train)
Xc_test_pre = preprocessor_c.transform(Xc_test)

Xc_train_dense = Xc_train_pre.toarray() if hasattr(Xc_train_pre, 'toarray') else Xc_train_pre
Xc_test_dense = Xc_test_pre.toarray() if hasattr(Xc_test_pre, 'toarray') else Xc_test_pre

print('Train shape:', Xc_train_dense.shape, '| Test shape:', Xc_test_dense.shape)

Train shape: (242, 28) | Test shape: (61, 28)

Train and test share the same encoded feature space; the exact width depends on one-hot expansion for rare categories.

In [ ]:
models_c = {
    'Naive Bayes': GaussianNB(),
    'Logistic Regression': LogisticRegression(max_iter=2000, random_state=RANDOM_STATE),
    'Decision Tree': DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=5, random_state=RANDOM_STATE),
    'Random Forest': RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=500, random_state=RANDOM_STATE),
    'Gradient Boosting': GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=RANDOM_STATE),
    'SVM RBF': SVC(C=2.0, kernel='rbf', probability=True, random_state=RANDOM_STATE),
    'MLP': MLPClassifier(hidden_layer_sizes=(64, 32), max_iter=1500, random_state=RANDOM_STATE),
    'Stacking Ensemble': StackingClassifier(
        estimators=[
            ('lr', LogisticRegression(max_iter=2000, random_state=RANDOM_STATE)),
            ('rf', RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=300, random_state=RANDOM_STATE)),
            ('svm', SVC(C=2.0, kernel='rbf', probability=True, random_state=RANDOM_STATE)),
        ],
        final_estimator=LogisticRegression(max_iter=2000, random_state=RANDOM_STATE),
        cv=5,
    ),
}

rows = []
fitted_c = {}
for name, model in models_c.items():
    model.fit(Xc_train_dense, yc_train)
    pred_train = model.predict(Xc_train_dense)
    pred_test = model.predict(Xc_test_dense)
    proba_train = model.predict_proba(Xc_train_dense)[:, 1]
    proba_test = model.predict_proba(Xc_test_dense)[:, 1]

    rows.append({
        'model': name,
        'train_acc': accuracy_score(yc_train, pred_train),
        'test_acc': accuracy_score(yc_test, pred_test),
        'train_auc': roc_auc_score(yc_train, proba_train),
        'test_auc': roc_auc_score(yc_test, proba_test),
        'test_f1': f1_score(yc_test, pred_test),
        'auc_gap_train_test': roc_auc_score(yc_train, proba_train) - roc_auc_score(yc_test, proba_test),
    })
    fitted_c[name] = model

bench_c = pd.DataFrame(rows).sort_values('test_auc', ascending=False)
bench_c
Out[ ]:
model train_acc test_acc train_auc test_auc test_f1 auc_gap_train_test
1 Logistic Regression 0.859504 0.885246 0.931229 0.966450 0.881356 -0.035221
6 MLP 1.000000 0.885246 1.000000 0.965368 0.885246 0.034632
5 SVM RBF 0.929752 0.901639 0.978131 0.964286 0.896552 0.013845
7 Stacking Ensemble 0.929752 0.868852 0.988928 0.963203 0.866667 0.025724
4 Gradient Boosting 0.995868 0.868852 0.999519 0.946970 0.866667 0.052549
3 Random Forest 1.000000 0.868852 1.000000 0.941558 0.866667 0.058442
0 Naive Bayes 0.747934 0.721311 0.904855 0.826840 0.746269 0.078015
2 Decision Tree 0.925620 0.737705 0.979609 0.745130 0.724138 0.234480

On this split, logistic regression is at the top on AUC while tree models can show a larger gap between train and test AUC. The table is the place to compare numbers.

In [ ]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 4))

plot_auc = bench_c.melt(id_vars='model', value_vars=['train_auc', 'test_auc'], var_name='split', value_name='auc')
sns.barplot(data=plot_auc, x='model', y='auc', hue='split', ax=axes[0])
axes[0].set_title('Cleveland: train vs test AUC')
axes[0].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=22)
axes[0].set_ylim(0.4, 1.05)

sns.barplot(data=bench_c, x='model', y='auc_gap_train_test', color='tomato', ax=axes[1])
axes[1].axhline(0, color='black', linewidth=1)
axes[1].set_title('Cleveland: train minus test AUC')
axes[1].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=22)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The plots above show the difference between the train and the test auc, to asses overfitting or underfitting.

We pick the best model by test AUC on this table and print the classification report on the held-out Cleveland patients.

In [21]:
best_c_name = bench_c.iloc[0]['model']
best_c_model = fitted_c[best_c_name]
best_c_pred = best_c_model.predict(Xc_test_dense)

print('Model with highest test AUC:', best_c_name)
print(classification_report(yc_test, best_c_pred, digits=3))

Model with highest test AUC: Logistic Regression
              precision    recall  f1-score   support

           0      0.933     0.848     0.889        33
           1      0.839     0.929     0.881        28

    accuracy                          0.885        61
   macro avg      0.886     0.889     0.885        61
weighted avg      0.890     0.885     0.885        61

The report gives precision and recall per class; useful if the clinical cost of false negatives differs from false positives.

3. Models (All sites)¶

Now we stack every processed centre into one table (full_processed already built above). Including site as a categorical input lets the model adjust for centre-specific effects during pooled training. That does not remove all shift-labels and protocols still differ; but it is a honest step before stricter checks.

We report three layers: a normal random split on the pooled data (optimistic if patients from the same hospital appear in both train and test), then GroupKFold with K=4 so each fold holds one entire site out for validation while training on the other three, and finally leave-one-site-out summaries (same idea as repeating training four times with a different held-out centre). We also plot calibration for the strongest robust model by mean AUC and look at error rates by age and sex on the full pooled feature matrix after fitting that model.

In [22]:
df_all = full_processed.copy()

X_m = df_all[[c for c in columns if c != 'num'] + ['site']].copy()
y_m = df_all['target_binary'].copy()
groups_m = df_all['site'].copy()

numeric_cols_m = numeric_cols
categorical_cols_m = categorical_cols + ['site']

num_pipe_m = Pipeline([
    ('imputer', SimpleImputer(strategy='median')),
    ('scaler', StandardScaler()),
])
cat_pipe_m = Pipeline([
    ('imputer', SimpleImputer(strategy='most_frequent')),
    ('onehot', OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown='ignore')),
])
preprocessor_m = ColumnTransformer([
    ('num', num_pipe_m, numeric_cols_m),
    ('cat', cat_pipe_m, categorical_cols_m),
])

models_m = {
    'Logistic Regression': LogisticRegression(max_iter=3000, random_state=RANDOM_STATE),
    'Random Forest': RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=500, random_state=RANDOM_STATE),
    'Gradient Boosting': GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=RANDOM_STATE),
    'SVM RBF': SVC(C=2.0, kernel='rbf', probability=True, random_state=RANDOM_STATE),
    'MLP': MLPClassifier(hidden_layer_sizes=(64, 32), max_iter=2000, random_state=RANDOM_STATE),
}

Xm_train, Xm_test, ym_train, ym_test = train_test_split(
    X_m, y_m, test_size=0.2, stratify=y_m, random_state=RANDOM_STATE
)

rows = []
for name, model in models_m.items():
    pipe = Pipeline([('prep', preprocessor_m), ('model', model)])
    pipe.fit(Xm_train, ym_train)
    pred = pipe.predict(Xm_test)
    proba = pipe.predict_proba(Xm_test)[:, 1]
    rows.append({
        'model': name,
        'test_acc': accuracy_score(ym_test, pred),
        'test_f1': f1_score(ym_test, pred),
        'test_auc': roc_auc_score(ym_test, proba),
        'brier': brier_score_loss(ym_test, proba),
    })

pooled_m = pd.DataFrame(rows).sort_values('test_auc', ascending=False)
pooled_m
Out[22]:
model test_acc test_f1 test_auc brier
1 Random Forest 0.842391 0.859903 0.927786 0.107338
0 Logistic Regression 0.842391 0.862559 0.925514 0.106678
2 Gradient Boosting 0.858696 0.877358 0.921330 0.106412
3 SVM RBF 0.853261 0.874419 0.914395 0.107698
4 MLP 0.831522 0.851675 0.889168 0.156944

Random mixing usually produces strong-looking AUC because the model can learn hospital-specific cues that partially leak between train and test.

Now we can tighten the estimate:

In [23]:
gkf = GroupKFold(n_splits=4)

group_rows = []
for name, model in models_m.items():
    pipe = Pipeline([('prep', preprocessor_m), ('model', model)])
    cv = cross_validate(
        pipe, X_m, y_m, groups=groups_m, cv=gkf,
        scoring=['roc_auc', 'f1', 'accuracy'], n_jobs=-1,
    )
    group_rows.append({
        'model': name,
        'gkf_auc_mean': cv['test_roc_auc'].mean(),
        'gkf_auc_std': cv['test_roc_auc'].std(),
        'gkf_f1_mean': cv['test_f1'].mean(),
        'gkf_acc_mean': cv['test_accuracy'].mean(),
    })

group_m = pd.DataFrame(group_rows).sort_values('gkf_auc_mean', ascending=False)
group_m.round(3)
Out[23]:
model gkf_auc_mean gkf_auc_std gkf_f1_mean gkf_acc_mean
0 Logistic Regression 0.801 0.077 0.764 0.716
3 SVM RBF 0.793 0.085 0.810 0.779
1 Random Forest 0.791 0.078 0.770 0.723
2 Gradient Boosting 0.781 0.067 0.736 0.697
4 MLP 0.745 0.086 0.749 0.707

Mean AUC here is typically lower than the pooled table because validation never shares patients with training within the same fold hospital. Standard deviation tells you how much performance swings when the held-out centre changes.

In [24]:
loso_rows = []
for test_site in sorted(df_all['site'].unique()):
    tr = df_all['site'] != test_site
    te = df_all['site'] == test_site
    Xtr, ytr = X_m.loc[tr], y_m.loc[tr]
    Xte, yte = X_m.loc[te], y_m.loc[te]

    for name, model in models_m.items():
        pipe = Pipeline([('prep', preprocessor_m), ('model', model)])
        pipe.fit(Xtr, ytr)
        pred = pipe.predict(Xte)
        proba = pipe.predict_proba(Xte)[:, 1]
        loso_rows.append({
            'heldout_site': test_site,
            'model': name,
            'auc': roc_auc_score(yte, proba),
            'f1': f1_score(yte, pred),
            'acc': accuracy_score(yte, pred),
        })

loso_m = pd.DataFrame(loso_rows)
robust_m = (
    loso_m.groupby('model')
    .agg(
        auc_mean=('auc', 'mean'),
        auc_std=('auc', 'std'),
        auc_min=('auc', 'min'),
        f1_mean=('f1', 'mean'),
        f1_min=('f1', 'min'),
    )
    .sort_values('auc_mean', ascending=False)
    .round(3)
)
robust_m
Out[24]:
auc_mean auc_std auc_min f1_mean f1_min
model
Logistic Regression 0.801 0.089 0.716 0.764 0.728
SVM RBF 0.793 0.099 0.695 0.810 0.768
Random Forest 0.791 0.090 0.708 0.770 0.739
Gradient Boosting 0.781 0.077 0.692 0.736 0.721
MLP 0.745 0.099 0.654 0.749 0.700

The minimum AUC column is the pessimistic line: it answers how bad the score can get when the test hospital is the hardest for that model. That matters more for deployment stories than the average alone.

In [ ]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))
plot_df = group_m.melt(id_vars='model', value_vars=['gkf_auc_mean', 'gkf_f1_mean', 'gkf_acc_mean'])
sns.barplot(data=plot_df, x='model', y='value', hue='variable', ax=axes[0])
axes[0].set_ylim(0.5, 1.0)
axes[0].set_title('GroupKFold means (one site out per fold)')
axes[0].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=18)

sns.boxplot(data=loso_m, x='model', y='auc', color='lightsteelblue', ax=axes[1])
axes[1].set_title('LOSO AUC distribution across held-out sites')
axes[1].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=18)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The plot above compares the models using two validation methods: GroupKFold and LOSO (Leave-One-Site-Out).

The left chart shows the average AUC, F1-score, and accuracy for each model. The right chart shows how the AUC changes across different held-out sites in LOSO validation.

We can understand that SVM RBF and Logistic Regression achieve the best and most stable performance. MLP is more variable, meaning it is less robust across sites. Overall, the models generalize reasonably well between different sites, with some variability caused by site differences.

In [26]:
best_m_name = robust_m.index[0]
best_m_model = models_m[best_m_name]
best_m_pipe = Pipeline([('prep', preprocessor_m), ('model', best_m_model)])

proba_oof = cross_val_predict(
    best_m_pipe, X_m, y_m, groups=groups_m, cv=gkf,
    method='predict_proba', n_jobs=-1,
)[:, 1]

frac_pos, mean_pred = calibration_curve(y_m, proba_oof, n_bins=10, strategy='quantile')

plt.figure(figsize=(5, 5))
plt.plot(mean_pred, frac_pos, marker='o', label=best_m_name)
plt.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], '--', color='gray', label='Perfect calibration')
plt.xlabel('Mean predicted probability')
plt.ylabel('Observed positive rate')
plt.title('Calibration (out-of-fold, grouped CV)')
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print('Model used:', best_m_name)
print('OOF Brier score:', round(brier_score_loss(y_m, proba_oof), 4))

Model used: Logistic Regression
OOF Brier score: 0.1773

If the curve sits close to the diagonal, predicted probabilities track observed frequencies within each bin. Large gaps would mean we should recalibrate before trusting risk numbers.

In [27]:
best_m_pipe.fit(X_m, y_m)
pred_all = (best_m_pipe.predict_proba(X_m)[:, 1] >= 0.5).astype(int)

err = X_m[['age', 'sex', 'site']].copy()
err['y_true'] = y_m.values
err['y_pred'] = pred_all
err['error'] = (err['y_true'] != err['y_pred']).astype(int)
err['age_group'] = pd.cut(err['age'], bins=[0, 45, 55, 65, 100], labels=['<=45', '46-55', '56-65', '66+'])

error_summary = (
    err.groupby(['age_group', 'sex'])['error']
    .mean()
    .reset_index()
    .rename(columns={'error': 'error_rate'})
)
error_summary
Out[27]:
age_group sex error_rate
0 <=45 0.0 0.020833
1 <=45 1.0 0.141892
2 46-55 0.0 0.120000
3 46-55 1.0 0.177686
4 56-65 0.0 0.214286
5 56-65 1.0 0.204461
6 66+ 0.0 0.066667
7 66+ 1.0 0.149254
In [28]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 4))
sns.barplot(data=error_summary, x='age_group', y='error_rate', hue='sex')
plt.ylim(0, 0.5)
plt.title('Misclassification rate by age band and sex (pooled fit)')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

This plot shows the misclassification rate across different age groups and sex categories.

The highest error rate appears in the 56–65 age group for both sexes, meaning the model has more difficulty classifying subjects in this range. Younger subjects (less than 45) show the lowest error rate, especially for sex 0 (females). Overall, sex 1 (males) tends to have slightly higher misclassification rates across most age groups.

4. Conclusions¶

On the Cleveland subset, Logistic Regression achieved the best test AUC (0.97), while SVM obtained the best accuracy and F1-score (around 0.90).

When all hospitals were combined, pooled train-test splits produced high AUC values between 0.91 and 0.93, especially for Random Forest, Logistic Regression, and Gradient Boosting. However, performance decreased when hospitals were separated during validation, showing the presence of domain shift between sites.

In the GroupKFold and LOSO evaluations, Logistic Regression achieved the highest mean AUC (0.801) and the best minimum LOSO AUC, indicating stronger generalization ability. SVM RBF achieved the highest mean F1-score (0.810) and showed very stable performance across sites.
